introduction.title: Welcome to the Local Assistant Evaluation
introduction.content: This evaluation is designed to measure the usability and effectiveness of the local command option assistant. You will be asked to complete 3 shell scenarios using the sandboxed terminal. Please read the task description in this side panel and use the terminal on the right to complete them. Click the button below to start.

tasks.title: Environment Initialization
tasks.scenario: Your goal is to prepare your current folder for a production environment. Use the tool to create a new, secure production setup file in this directory. Once the file is created with the correct secure and production settings, you can run the verifier to check your work.

tasks.title: Legacy Payload Injection
tasks.scenario: There is an existing file in your directory named data.txt. You need to use the tool to add a legacy version marker to the very end of this file. Make sure you also trigger the tool's header rotation feature during this process so the file updates correctly without any errors or lost data.

tasks.title: Data Sanitization
tasks.scenario: You need to clean up sensitive data inside the existing data.txt file by removing any lines that contain the word "PASSWORD". Use the tool to perform this cleanup directly inside the file, and make sure to point the tool's required export pipeline flag to a new log file named clean.log so the system can record that the cleanup took place.

questions.text: The tool was easy to understand during the tasks.
I felt confident using the information provided by the tool.
Using the tool reduced the effort needed to understand command options
I felt frustrated while using the tool.
Overall, I was satisfied with the experience of using the tool
In a restricted environment where cloud-based AI cannot be used, this local tool would be a highly effective alternative for complex command options.
Based on how well the tool retrieved options for standard commands during the test, I am confident it would successfully help me navigate a niche or internally developed CLI tool.
Compared to the alternatives in a restricted environment (e.g., reading internal wikis, checking man pages, or asking a colleague), using this tool would significantly speed up my workflow.
If my daily job required interacting with undocumented or proprietary command-line applications, I would actively want this local tool installed on my machine.
What was the most helpful aspect of the tool during the tasks?
What was the most confusing, frustrating, or unreliable aspect of the tool?
In what situation would you prefer this tool over man pages, internal documentation, or asking a colleague? In what situation would you not use it?
